CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T88 (goals:G4) — "Bind source->peer only via authenticated PROBE with trial-decode across peer PSKs"
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a8451cdfaa8267fa3 (branch implement/T88, base 8c0411d)
Go repo — gate incl. `go test -race ./internal/bind/...` via nix develop.
acceptance: Unit test: a PROBE encoded under peer B's psk from a fresh source binds that source to B (and reflects an echo); a forged/garbage frame verifies under no peer psk and establishes no binding; trial-decode stops at the first matching psk. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.
Worker: pass; commit 5e29b69; files internal/bind/{multipath.go, reconcile.go, peer_fanout_test.go, source_bind_test.go(new)}. Design: readLoop-only demuxInbound wrapper (handleInbound per-view contract byte-identical); peerBySource is a lock-free copy-on-write atomic.Pointer map with CAS binding (readers must never take m.mu — Close holds it while waiting on readers); per-socket views published copy-on-write on sharedPathState; <=1-view fast path unchanged; unbound authenticated DATA/PARITY dropped (no binding authority); D9/D11 preserved.
Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.

Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

diff --git a/internal/bind/multipath.go b/internal/bind/multipath.go
index 99a104c..eb41b17 100644
--- a/internal/bind/multipath.go
+++ b/internal/bind/multipath.go
@@ -86,6 +86,33 @@ type sharedPathState struct {
 	id   uint8
 	src  netip.Addr
 	conn *net.UDPConn
+
+	// views is the per-peer VIEW set of THIS shared socket — one peerPathState per bound
+	// peer — published copy-on-write through an atomic.Pointer so the single Bind-owned
+	// readLoop demuxes an inbound datagram to its owning peer's view WITHOUT m.mu on the
+	// receive hot path (the same lock-free-publish discipline peersView/resequencer use).
+	// It is (re)published under m.mu at every fan-out site (Open / attachPeerPathLocked /
+	// deferred promote, and each concentrator peer bind). On the single-peer edge/hub it
+	// holds exactly one entry (the primary's view), which handleInbound reads as "no demux
+	// needed" — the byte-identical fast path. len(views)>1 marks a shared concentrator
+	// socket whose datagrams must be source-demuxed to the owning peer (T88).
+	views atomic.Pointer[[]*peerPathState]
+}
+
+// addViewLocked publishes pp as a per-peer view of this shared socket for the lock-free
+// receive demux (T88). Copy-on-write: it republishes a NEW slice with pp appended, so a
+// readLoop mid-iteration over the previously-published snapshot is never disturbed. The
+// caller holds m.mu (every fan-out site does), which serializes writers; readers Load
+// without a lock.
+func (sp *sharedPathState) addViewLocked(pp *peerPathState) {
+	old := sp.views.Load()
+	var next []*peerPathState
+	if old != nil {
+		next = make([]*peerPathState, len(*old), len(*old)+1)
+		copy(next, *old)
+	}
+	next = append(next, pp)
+	sp.views.Store(&next)
 }
 
 // peerPathState is one peer's per-(peer,path) VIEW of a shared uplink: that peer's own
@@ -319,13 +346,21 @@ type Multipath struct {
 	// receive hot path — the same lock-free-publish discipline resequencer/fecRecv use. It
 	// is never nil after construction (the constructor publishes the primary-only view).
 	peersView atomic.Pointer[[]*peerState]
-	// peerByEndpoint / peerBySource are the inbound demux lookup maps the concentrator's
-	// shared-socket receive path needs to route a datagram to the owning peer (endpoint or
-	// learned source → peer). They are PLACEHOLDERS today: the single-peer edge/hub needs no
-	// demux (one peer owns every socket), so they stay empty; the concentrator receive path
-	// (a later G4 task) populates and reads them.
+	// peerByEndpoint is the inbound endpoint-keyed demux placeholder (still unused today: the
+	// single-peer edge/hub needs no demux, and the source-keyed binding below is what the
+	// concentrator receive path routes on).
 	peerByEndpoint map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState
-	peerBySource   map[netip.Addr]*peerState
+	// peerBySource is the inbound source-demux map: a learned source address → the peer it was
+	// bound to by an authenticated PROBE (T88). The concentrator's per-socket readLoops resolve
+	// a datagram's source to its owning peer through it; a source is bound ONLY on the first
+	// PROBE that MAC-verifies under a peer's psk (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned
+	// only from authenticated PROBEs). It is published copy-on-write through an atomic.Pointer
+	// so the receive hot path resolves a bound source with a lock-free Load (no m.mu — a reader
+	// must never block on m.mu, since Close waits on the readers WHILE holding it), and a new
+	// binding is installed lock-free by a CAS republish of a copy with the entry added
+	// (bindSourceToPeer). Nil until the first binding; the single-peer edge/hub never consults
+	// it (one peer owns every socket — handleInbound's fast path skips the demux entirely).
+	peerBySource atomic.Pointer[map[netip.Addr]*peerState]
 	// peerByVirt routes an OUTBOUND Send to its owning peer: the engine hands Send the
 	// single virtual endpoint (*udpEndpoint) it holds for a peer, and this map resolves
 	// that pointer to the peer's datapath state (outerSeq, scheduler, sendCodec, fecSend,
@@ -513,7 +548,6 @@ func NewMultipath(paths []config.Path, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler
 		peers:          []*peerState{primary},
 		peersByName:    map[string]*peerState{primary.name: primary},
 		peerByEndpoint: map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState{},
-		peerBySource:   map[netip.Addr]*peerState{},
 		peerByVirt:     map[*udpEndpoint]*peerState{primary.virt: primary},
 		fecCfg:         fecCfg,
 		adaptiveCfg:    adaptiveCfg,
@@ -658,6 +692,10 @@ func (m *Multipath) Open(port uint16) ([]ReceiveFunc, uint16, error) {
 		}
 		m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
 		m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
+		// Publish the primary's view for the receive demux (T88). On the single-peer edge/hub
+		// this is the ONLY view, which handleInbound reads as the byte-identical fast path; a
+		// concentrator peer bound later appends its own view to the same socket.
+		shared.addViewLocked(ps)
 		if firstBound {
 			actualPort = uint16(c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).Port)
 			firstBound = false
@@ -841,7 +879,7 @@ func (m *Multipath) readLoop(ps *peerPathState, deliver chan<- struct{}) {
 		// pulled off its socket before dispatch, lock-free. This goroutine is the sole
 		// writer of ps.rxBytes, so the atomic Add is uncontended.
 		ps.rxBytes.Add(uint64(n))
-		m.handleInbound(ps, readBuf[:n], srcAP)
+		m.demuxInbound(ps, readBuf[:n], srcAP)
 		// Advance liveness off the receive path (throttled): a live signal on THIS path
 		// is what lets a DIFFERENT, silent path be marked DOWN promptly even when the
 		// probe-loop ticker is starved under load (D15). The peer probes every path
@@ -1032,9 +1070,12 @@ func (m *Multipath) newReceiveFunc(deliver <-chan struct{}, closed <-chan struct
 	}
 }
 
-// handleInbound decodes one received outer datagram and dispatches it by kind. It
-// is the single per-frame receive action, factored out of readLoop so the probe
-// transport is exercisable without spinning receive goroutines. Delivery up the WG
+// handleInbound decodes one received outer datagram UNDER THIS VIEW'S CODEC and dispatches
+// it by kind to the view's owning peer. It is the single per-frame receive action; the
+// readLoop reaches it through demuxInbound, which first resolves the owning view on a shared
+// concentrator socket (on the single-peer edge/hub the reader's own view is the owner, so
+// demuxInbound's fast path calls this directly — byte-identical to the pre-concentrator
+// behaviour). Delivery up the WG
 // path is deferred to the resequencer (Pop, in the engine-facing drainer): a DATA
 // frame is not handed up here but pushed into the shared resequencer to be released in
 // outer-seq order.
@@ -1068,10 +1109,113 @@ func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.Add
 	if err != nil {
 		return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
 	}
-	// Route to the OWNING peer's resequencer / FEC decoder / reflector: the per-(peer,path)
-	// state (ps) back-references its peerState, so a shared socket serving many peers still
-	// resequences each peer's stream against that peer's own buffer. On the single-peer
-	// edge/hub ps.peer is the embedded primary, so this is byte-identical to the singleton.
+	m.dispatchInbound(ps, fr, raw, srcAP)
+}
+
+// demuxInbound is the readLoop's per-frame entry on a (possibly shared) socket: it resolves
+// which bound peer's view owns the datagram, then hands the frame to handleInbound on THAT
+// view (T88). Because the readLoop holds only the PRIMARY's view of a shared socket (one
+// reader per socket), the routing that a concentrator needs — a datagram from peer B decoded
+// and resequenced under peer B's plane — happens HERE, not by trusting the reader's own view.
+//
+// Fast path: a socket with a single peer view (the edge/hub, or any socket not shared by a
+// second peer) needs no demux — dispatch on the reader's own view, byte-identical to the
+// pre-concentrator behaviour. The per-socket view snapshot is read lock-free; a nil/one-entry
+// snapshot means "not a shared concentrator socket".
+//
+// Shared socket (>1 view):
+//   - A source already bound by a prior authenticated PROBE routes straight to that peer's
+//     view of THIS socket (one lock-free map Load). A frame that does not verify under that
+//     peer's psk — a spoofed source it cannot forge the MAC for — is dropped by handleInbound.
+//   - An unbound source is trial-decoded against each peer's psk-derived codec (O(peers),
+//     bounded by the static peer count). A genuine frame authenticates under EXACTLY ONE psk,
+//     so the first MAC that verifies identifies the peer and the loop STOPS there. Only an
+//     authenticated PROBE establishes a binding (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned
+//     only from authenticated PROBEs); an authenticated DATA/PARITY from a not-yet-bound
+//     source carries no binding authority and is dropped until the peer's PROBE binds it. A
+//     forged/garbage frame verifies under NO psk, binds nothing, and is dropped cheaply.
+func (m *Multipath) demuxInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
+	views := ps.views.Load()
+	if views == nil || len(*views) <= 1 {
+		m.handleInbound(ps, raw, srcAP)
+		return
+	}
+	if bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(srcAP.Addr()); ok {
+		for _, v := range *views {
+			if v.peer == bound {
+				m.handleInbound(v, raw, srcAP)
+				return
+			}
+		}
+		return // the bound peer holds no view of this socket (removed): drop
+	}
+	for _, v := range *views {

280	// double-init'd ChaCha20 (once in peekByte, once for the full body) on EVERY
281	// frame — prohibitive on the per-datagram datapath the multipath Bind drives.
282	//
283	// A Codec is NOT safe for concurrent use: its scratch buffers are shared across
284	// calls. Construct one per goroutine (the Bind gives each per-path receive loop
285	// its own Codec and guards the shared send Codec with the Bind mutex).
286	type Codec struct {
287		obfKey     []byte
288		authKey    []byte
289		encScratch []byte // reused body buffer for Encode
290		decScratch []byte // reused body buffer for Decode
291	}
292	
293	// NewCodec derives the PSK-bound subkeys once and returns a reusable Codec. It
294	// fails only if the PSK is unset.
295	func NewCodec(psk config.Key) (*Codec, error) {
296		obfKey, authKey, err := subkeys(psk)
297		if err != nil {
298			return nil, err
299		}
300		return &Codec{obfKey: obfKey, authKey: authKey}, nil
301	}
302	
303	// Encode appends the wire encoding of f to dst and returns the extended slice,
304	// letting the caller reuse one buffer across sends (pass dst[:0]). It fails only
305	// if the system CSPRNG is unavailable.
306	func (c *Codec) Encode(dst []byte, f Frame) ([]byte, error) {
307		// Build the plaintext body (kind || header || payload) in reusable scratch.
308		c.encScratch = f.appendBody(c.encScratch[:0])
309		body := c.encScratch
310	
311		start := len(dst)
312		dst = append(dst, make([]byte, nonceLen)...)
313		nonce := dst[start : start+nonceLen]
314		if _, err := rand.Read(nonce); err != nil {
315			return nil, fmt.Errorf("frame: read nonce: %w", err)
316		}
317	
318		// Obfuscate the body in scratch, then append it; a single keystream init.
319		obfuscate(c.obfKey, nonce, body)
320		dst = append(dst, body...)
321	
322		if f.Kind().authenticated() {
323			// After the append above, nonce and obfBody are contiguous in dst's
324			// (possibly reallocated) backing array; tag over nonce||obfBody.
325			nonce = dst[start : start+nonceLen]
326			obfBody := dst[start+nonceLen:]
327			dst = append(dst, tag(c.authKey, nonce, obfBody)...)
328		}
329		return dst, nil
330	}
331	
332	// Decode parses a wire frame. It verifies the MAC of authenticated frames and
333	// rejects tampered or PSK-mismatched ones. It never panics on malformed input.
334	// The returned frame's payload is a fresh copy, so the caller may reuse raw and
335	// the Codec's scratch immediately.
336	//
337	// Authentication guarantee: a frame is accepted as an authenticated kind
338	// (CONTROL/PROBE) only if its MAC verifies under the PSK, so tampered or
339	// PSK-mismatched authenticated frames are rejected. Flipping the (obfuscated)
340	// kind byte can re-label an authenticated frame as an unauthenticated kind
341	// (DATA/PARITY), which then decodes without a MAC — this is not a downgrade
342	// break, because DATA/PARITY are forgeable by design (the inner WireGuard layer
343	// authenticates the real payload). No mutation can make a frame decode as an
344	// authentic CONTROL/PROBE.
345	func (c *Codec) Decode(raw []byte) (Frame, error) {
346		// Need at least the nonce plus one body byte (the kind discriminant).
347		if len(raw) < nonceLen+1 {
348			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: %d bytes, need >= %d", ErrMalformed, len(raw), nonceLen+1)
349		}
350	
351		nonce := raw[:nonceLen]
352		rest := raw[nonceLen:]
353	
354		// One keystream for the whole decode: init the cipher once, consume the
355		// first keystream byte to recover the (obfuscated) kind, then continue the
356		// SAME keystream over the remaining body. This avoids the old peekByte's
357		// second ChaCha20 init.
358		cipher, err := chacha20.NewUnauthenticatedCipher(c.obfKey, nonce)
359		if err != nil {
360			// obfKey is subkeyLen and nonce is nonceLen, so init cannot fail; a
361			// failure indicates a programmer error.
362			panic(fmt.Sprintf("frame: chacha20 init: %v", err))
363		}
364		kindByte := []byte{rest[0]}
365		cipher.XORKeyStream(kindByte, kindByte)
366		kind := Kind(kindByte[0])
367		if !kind.valid() {
368			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: unknown kind %d", ErrMalformed, uint8(kind))
369		}
370	
371		obfBody := rest
372		if kind.authenticated() {
373			if len(rest) < tagLen+1 {
374				return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: authenticated frame too short", ErrMalformed)
375			}
376			obfBody = rest[:len(rest)-tagLen]
377			gotTag := rest[len(rest)-tagLen:]
378			wantTag := tag(c.authKey, nonce, obfBody)
379			if !hmac.Equal(gotTag, wantTag) {
380				return nil, ErrAuth
381			}
382		}
383	
384		// Reconstruct the plaintext body in reusable scratch. Byte 0 is the kind we
385		// already recovered; the remaining bytes continue the same keystream.
386		c.decScratch = append(c.decScratch[:0], obfBody...)
387		body := c.decScratch
388		body[0] = kindByte[0]
389		if len(body) > 1 {
390			cipher.XORKeyStream(body[1:], body[1:])
391		}
392		return decodeBody(kind, body[1:])
393	}

397	// datapath uses a long-lived Codec instead (see Codec / defect D5). It fails
398	// only if the PSK is unset or the system CSPRNG is unavailable.
399	func Encode(psk config.Key, f Frame) ([]byte, error) {

  - `frame.Codec.Decode` is stateless w.r.t. anti-replay (mutates only per-codec `decScratch`, returns a fresh-copy payload), so trial-decoding one datagram across every peer's codec cannot corrupt replay/nonce state. A wrong-psk PROBE is rejected at MAC (`ErrAuth`); a wrong-psk unauthenticated DATA/PARITY at worst decodes to garbage but is dropped (non-PROBE → no binding), so misattribution is impossible for anything that establishes state.
